title: Gluten-free digestive biscuits
subtitle: The familiar malty biscuit made with oat flour and rice flour
excerpt: Gluten-free digestives that replicate the wheaty, malty flavour of the original by using a blend of certified gluten-free oat flour and rice flour. The texture is slightly more crumbly than a wheat biscuit, but the characteristic sandy crunch is there.
sourceNotes: The digestive biscuit formula is documented in British baking tradition from the late nineteenth century. This gluten-free adaptation follows the standard formula but replaces wheat flour with a blend of oat flour and rice flour, following established gluten-free baking practice.

recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe; bake in batches on separate trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 5 days. Gluten-free biscuits soften faster than wheat ones; a tin is essential.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 170 degrees C; watch closely as gluten-free biscuits can colour suddenly

glossaryTerms.definition: Without gluten to provide structure and snap, gluten-free biscuits rely on the fat and sugar setting around the starch granules during baking. Oat flour contributes the characteristic slightly malty flavour that makes a digestive recognisable; rice flour adds a fine, sandy texture. The mixture must be chilled before rolling so the fat stays solid; rolling warm gluten-free dough produces sticky, uneven results.

content.text: relies on chilling the dough before rolling. The oats must be certified gluten-free; standard oats are often contaminated with wheat during processing. Oat flour can be made by blitzing certified gluten-free rolled oats in a food processor until fine. The biscuits are done when they look and feel just set; they firm up significantly as they cool.

Combine the oat flour, rice flour, caster sugar, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the cold milk and press the dough together; it will be slightly stiff. Flatten into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 190 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the cold dough between two sheets of baking paper to about 3 mm thick; gluten-free dough benefits from rolling between sheets to prevent sticking. Cut into rounds using a 7 cm cutter and transfer carefully to the trays. Prick each biscuit several times with a fork. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the edges are just starting to colour; the centres will still look barely set. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes to firm before transferring to a rack. They will be crisp when fully cooled.
